comments.title: Sacred Geometry
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I am an architect and have for the past few years fallen down the rabbit hole of classical systems of geometry. I took a course in classical proportion systems and my mind was blown when the discussion turned to neo-Platonism and other occult topics. For the first time I could begin to see a wedding of my profession with my interest in occult subjects. I sense a deeper meaning in the geometry that I am immersed in daily. I feel it lurking just under the pencil. I am curious what it could mean away from drawing as well. Any books you care to recommend?

Thanks as ever,

Black Birch

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content:
Fortunately there's been quite a bit published on sacred geometry over the last few decades. Robert Lawlor's Sacred Geometry: Principles and Practice is a good introduction; Matila Ghyka's The Geometry of Art and Life and The Golden Number, and John Michell's How the World is Made are other worthwhile resources, and the works of Jay Hambidge might be particularly useful to you as an architect.

Did your course on proportion by any chance reference Richard Padovan's fine book Proportion: Science, Philosophy, Architecture? If not, I recommend it highly.

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content:
Thanks for the recommendations! Lawlor was on the list from the course  but I found it inexplicably opaque. Michell was there but a different book. Ghyka and Padovan are new and I will check them out!

I am curious to find a spiritual dimmension to geometry. I heard a podcast of yours recently where you mentioned scared geometry as a metaphor (and practice?) for addressing the incomeasurables of life. Does Freemasonry address this?

Thanks again!

F

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content: Lawlor's book needs to be taken a little at a time -- it's not a quick or easy read. The workbook exercises are the most useful thing in it. As for Freemasonry, that's a complex question. Sacred geometry is hardwired into the structure of the Craft, as we Masons call our venerable and quirky institution, but the vast majority of Masons have no idea that it's there and are given no instruction in finding it.

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content:
You might also enjoy “The Return of Sacred Architecture: the Golden Ratio and the End of Modernism” by Herbert Bangs, an architect who is also Past Master of his Masonic Lodge.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content: Hmm! I'm not familiar with that book, and clearly need to change that. Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content:
Hello Kevin,

Thank you for the recommendation! I chuckled when I saw IM Pei’s pyramid on the cover of the book. Until recently so worked at Pei Cobb Freed. While I never worked with Mr. Pei. I did have the great fortune to interact with Harry Cobb. He fed my interest in classical proportion systems. I actually ended up designing and fabricating a proportional divider that calculates all the root proportions. It’s the only tool of its kind as far as I can tell.

If you can say, is sacred geometry addresses in Frenasonry? My grandfather was a Freemason but passed before I was able to speak with him about these things.

Thanks again,

Black Birch

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content: I'd also suggest taking a look at some of Randall Carlson's youtube videos. He has a number of them about ancient geometry. He is well spoken and fascinating to listen to.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Do you know of any workings to help with sleeping? I seem to have a very hard time getting to sleep, and nothing mundane has worked yet....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Trouble sleeping can have many different causes, some of which can be affected by magic and some of which can't. Do you have any sense of what it is that's interfering with your sleep?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm not sure.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's going to make it a little more difficult, but there are ways around it. Two things I'd advise:

1) Start keeping a sleep diary. Each morning, note how hard it was to get to sleep and how long you slept, and then note down sources of stress during the day before, what you ate for dinner, what you did between dinner and bed, and anything else that might be relevant. Look for common patterns.

2) Begin journaling. Get a blank notebook, and imagine yourself having a conversation with sleep. Ask it questions, writing them down, and then write down the answers that come to you, no matter how absurd or weird they seem. Do this regularly and see what surfaces.

Both of those are ways to get at the causes of your sleep problems, the first by figuring out if there's a lifestyle component, the second by getting access to the subconscious and seeing what it has to say. Once you have some sense of the cause(s), you can start moving toward a solution.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Okay, thank you for the advice.

replies.title: Sleeping
replies.content:
Hi,
I have always had a hard time getting to sleep until I stopped using electric lights at night. I read and dine by candlelight and as soon as my head hits the pillow, I sleep well. This also means no screen time in the evening.

I learned about this from the physician who discovered seasonal affective disorder.

replies.title: Re: Sleeping
replies.content:
Good point. E-screens emit light mostly toward the blue end of the visible spectrum which tricks your mind into thinking it's morning and you should be wide awake. It's more difficult in winter for sure, but I try to cut way back on screen time after sunset. Just a thought...

Tripp

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I can't help on the magic front, but I'm a veteran insomniac. There are a few things that've helped me:

1) stay off screens after sunset. But if you absolutely can't avoid it, there's a lovely piece of free software called "flux" that you can install, that will suck most of the blue tones out of your computer display at sunset. It helps a bit.

2) If you're sleeping on a normal bed, consider ditching your box springs. You can get a basic no-box-springs-needed platform bed for under $200 (also not difficult to make, if you know your way around a toolbox). You can try before you spend money by sliding some plywood between the mattress and the box springs, or just putting the mattress on the floor for a few days (not a good long term solution as it tends to grow mold this way) . We made the switch a week ago, and the difference is startling. I had forgotten what it was like to dream every night. Next we plan to trade out the old innerspring mattress for a futon-- one more baby step down the less-tech-less-problems path.

3) See if you can train yourself out of using a pillow. I was forced to do this by a pinched nerve in my neck. But it's helped a lot with overall sleep quality. I still have a folded bath towel to rest my head on (more psychological appeasement than necessity, I think-- pillows were hard to give up).

Apologies if you've tried it all already. Been there, done that.

comments.title: Coelbren; animals
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I recently got back to Beijing after a trip home to Wales. On my last trip, I bought a copy of your Coelbren book; this time round, I bought a pack of wooden Scrabble tiles, a black Sharpie, and a leather drawstring bag, and made myself a set of my own. I'm still getting used to using it, with your book, plus the original ab Ithel text, but I'm finding it to be an accurate, insightful, and versatile tool. Thanks for bringing it out of obscurity!

I guess you know about the error on page 100? The symbol is supposed to be “Fi”, but is actually “Ffi”, which was introduced on page 76. This was fairly easy to spot, but is there anything else readers should be aware of? I'd already made the extra tile before I noticed, but it's now on my home altar: 'Ffi', in its sense of "Nothing will happen unless you make it happen", is really the story of my life...

I was going to ask a question about the Coelbren, but I think I need to study ab Ithel's guide to the meanings more closely first.

Instead, I have another question: while I was in Wales, I had close encounters with, separately, a fox, an owl, and a hawk. Bear in mind that this was in the town where I grew up and also where I've lived many years as an adult. To be as close as I was to any one of these creatures is unprecedented; to be so close to three in the space of the week tells me to look for a message. Animal symbolism is not something I've ever worked with, and the sites I find online seem to be a New Agey mishmash of world mythology and made-up stuff. Can you - or other readers - suggest a reliable guide, ideally with a Celtic/Welsh focus?

Oh, one other question, if you don't mind: do you happen to know of an offering that would be particularly pleasing to the Plant Llyr?

Once again, I add my thanks to those of others for the guidance and advice you provide here...

Bogatyr

replies.title: Re: Coelbren; animals
replies.content:
Delighted to hear that you're finding the Coelbren useful! Yes, I know about the error; as far as I know that's the only one that slipped by.

I don't know of a good concise source for Celtic animal lore that's worth the name. The one thing I know that seems to work fairly well in this context isn't traditional -- it's Philip Carr-Gomm's Druid Animal Oracle, which is based on a free mix of folklore and visionary material. A mishmosh? Sure, but it gives accurate answers.

As for offerings to the Plant Llyr, I've always used libations of mead, but there are probably other options as well.

replies.title: Re: Coelbren; animals
replies.content:
For what it's worth, I use the Carr-Gomm's Druid Animal Oracle and get wonderful results from it! And curiously, I had a run-in with the same 3 animals in a short span of time recently too!! Hmmm...

Cheers,
Tripp

replies.title: Re: Coelbren; animals
replies.content: Hmm, thanks. Neither the cards nor mead are easily available here in China; guess I'll have to wait until my next trip to Wales!

comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,

After last week's answer you gave me on praying to Hermes to help with a relocation, I was wondering how does one go about praying to other gods in a similair ritual setting? Is there some way of determining the day and candle colour required when praying to different gods?

I was also wondering if I should just blow out the candle when the prayer is done or is there some other way to conclude the proceedings?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The way most ceremonial magicians do it is to assign candle colors by the planets, which of course also relate to the days of the week:

Sun - yellow - Sunday - solar deities, or deities who die and rise again
Moon - violet - Monday - deities of water and the Moon
Mars - red - Tuesday - deities of war and protection
Mercury - orange - Wednesday - deities of travel, trade, and intellect
Jupiter - blue - Thursday - sky and thunder deities
Venus - green - Friday - deities of love and wealth
Saturn - black - Saturday - deities of agriculture, time, and death

Some people just blow the candle out, others use a snuffer.

comments.title: Incorporating the Etheric into Western Magic
comments.content:
I recall you mentioning in one of your books (Circles of Power, IIRC), that Western Magic has largely concerned itself with astral forms of magic over the etheric.

In your opinion, are there any forms of Western magic that are more etheric in nature than astral? How would one go about adding an etheric element to one's magic?

In somewhat related matters, I wanted to say that I agree with your previous statements regarding the planetary Intelligiences being "blind forces" rather than evil. I find that they work for mundane effects although the results they bring can be somewhat questionable or short-lived, e.g., Hismael will bring sums of money when petitioned for "prosperity", but it will generally happen in the most direct and short-lived way possible.

Prosperity that is long-lived still requires some sort of intelligence guiding it (either the planetary or one's own). It almost seems as if the intelligence has an extremely low attention span and requires constant attention for longer lasting results.

Thanks again for these Magic Mondays!

replies.title: Re: Incorporating the Etheric into Western Magic
replies.content:
The central European magical traditions that built on Franz Anton Mesmer's work are heavily involved in etheric work. The only detailed text from that end of magic I know of in English is Franz Bardon's Initiation into Hermetics. The etheric needs to be handled with quite some care, since it deals with the life energies -- you can mess up your health badly if you don't know what you're doing. Bardon's approach seems to be safe, though.

I believe I said that the planetary spirits, not the intelligences, were blind forces. The intelligences are not, but they're also not particularly attuned to the broader picture. This is why it's standard in GD practice to start by invoking the divine name governing an operation, then move to the archangel, then to the angel, then to the intelligence, and finally to the spirit. That way you get results that are part of the whole pattern of the cosmos, and therefore endure.

replies.title: Re: Incorporating the Etheric into Western Magic
replies.content: Do rituals such as the banishing ritual of the pentagram work on the etheric as well as the astral planes?

replies.title: Re: Incorporating the Etheric into Western Magic
replies.content: They affect the etheric plane indirectly. Each plane shapes the plane immediately beneath it, so when you act on the astral, you affect the etheric.

replies.title: Re: Incorporating the Etheric into Western Magic
replies.content:
That would seem to suggest that action on the material plane can affect demons on the plane below. For better or for worse. Is that off target? Is this why physical objects like horseshoes, gargoyles, or hematite are used to "ward off evil"? Mark me down for a post on demons too!

Tripp

comments.title: Planes and subtle bodies in CGD
comments.content:
Dear John,
As part of the CGD Second Knowledge Lecture, I’ve been studying and meditating on the three circles in connection to the planes and subtle bodies. You have mentioned elsewhere that there are many different ways of ‘dividing’ the planes, depending on different traditions but a sort of common or ‘traditional’ way posits 5 planes, the material, the etheric, the astral, the mental and the spiritual. In the CGD system you talk of three planes (material, aetherial, intellectual) corresponding to the three circles of existence: if these are the material, astral and mental of the 5 plane system, are the realities implied by the etheric and spiritual traditional planes somehow also present in the CGD view? After some study and meditation, I came to view the etheric (not the aethereal!) as belonging to Abred but on the cusp of Gwynfydd (being the interface between the material and the astral/aetherial) while, in a bit of a mirror way, the intellectual plane corresponding to the ‘regions’ of Ceugant closer to Gwynfydd, leaving the spiritual plane in the higher regions of Ceugant. Does this make any sense or am I getting the wrong end of the wand here?
Many thanks again!
Manuel

replies.title: Re: Planes and subtle bodies in CGD
replies.content: No, you've pretty much got the wand by the right end. It's actually quite common in occult philosophy to consider the physical and the etheric to be parts of one plane, the material plane of the CGD system; the aetherial plane of the CGD system is the astral plane; and the mental and spiritual planes make up the intellectual plane of the CGD system.

comments.content:
You commented some time ago that parts of the information on demons in Monsters are outdated, because you learned a bit on them since the original edition was wrote. You are no longer in the field of monster hunting, but what are the most important points that changed? The book states that demons are from a plane "below the material" and that they link with us through the astral plane. Is this part still accurate?

Finally, I want to thank you for the Monsters book. It is one of my favorites, and it was the entry point to magic for me. On a strange way, monster hunting is something I would never do. replies.title: Re: Demons
replies.content: John, did you ever catch a monster?  If so, what happened?

replies.title: Re: Demons
replies.content:
Nope. My practical monster hunting was restricted to investigating a couple of haunted places, on the one hand, and locating certain Native American sacred places in Seattle, on the other. Mostly I was interested in the history of Fortean events in the Seattle area, so did a lot of research in books and old newspapers. I had quite a collection before we moved.

The ghost investigations were made more congenial than they might have been by the fact that the haunted places in question were old lodge buildings, and I belonged to the relevant lodges. (I've yet to encounter a really old lodge building that didn't have some of the former brethren as permanent residents.) In the Ballard Odd Fellows Hall, which I managed for some years, late at night when the hall upstairs was closed and locked, and I was downstairs, I (and others) would hear footfalls on the floor above, and it got to the point that I could figure out where they were in the process of opening or closing the lodge...

replies.title: Re: Demons
replies.content: I'd say rather that demons come from the plane below us, but make contact with us through the sphere of sensation, which is the astral and etheric structure more generally called the aura. I'll consider a post on them.

replies.title: Re: Demons
replies.content:
I'm also interested in the post on demons!

In the mean time, would you be willing to shed some light on a couple of points about them I've been puzzling over for a while?

First, how do demons fit into reincarnation as you've described it? Does being from the plane below ours mean that they'll eventually come up to our plane, or are they on a separate track?

Second, the terminology about planes has me a little confused. Does the word "plane" as in the "demon plane" and the "human plane" refer to the same striations of existence as the word "plane" in the terms "astral plane" and "etheric plane?"
It sounds a little strange to think that the beings from the plane below ours make contact with us through the part of our body that exists in the plane above ours, but maybe I'm just picturing the whole thing wrong.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Demons
replies.content:
1) Demons are on a second track. When one of them gets incarnated as a human being -- according to some writers, this happens now and again -- something has gone very, very wrong.

2) Yes, the planes are the same in both cases. The astral plane is the plane on which we have our inner lives -- it's the plane of thoughts and wishes and fantasies and dreams -- and the factor that makes us vulnerable to contact with demons is the same as the factor that keeps so many people's inner lives so rigidly fixed on the material plane.

replies.title: Re: Demons
replies.content: Which factor is that, precisely...?

replies.title: Re: Demons
replies.content: The Golden Dawn knowledge lectures call it "material inclination.' It's a fixation of consciousness on the material plane to the exclusion of other, more important planes.

comments.title: Questions
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

Not so long ago I had a crystal break through mishandling and I've been trying to figure out what to do with it. I meditated on the issue and came up some ideas: The crystal point itself was once part of a larger mass so the breaking doesn't ruin it, though the function that the crystal once served is the the same as the pieces. I could potentially 'repair' the crystal with glass glue, in sort of a wabi sabi fashion, and it wouldn't be much different if somebody had originally included the crystal as part of a wand or other design.

The lore that I had received from some folks a long time ago was that broken crystals should be buried since they were 'used up'. That has seemed more and more nonsensical and consumerist as I have thought about it over the years. I was wondering if you had any more insight on how to handle broken crystals.

One more thing that had come up in my meditation: is there something analogous to a compost heap for the gods? Following the discussion around Fortune's Cosmic Doctrine makes me think that those ten-sided atoms just keep 'developing' forever. If the 'as above, so below' adage is correct, it would seem like they have ancestors of their own or ... maybe divine loam that they draw from?

Thank you for your insight,
-Truly

replies.title: Re: Questions
replies.content:
I've never done much with crystals -- that's specifically a New Age thing, not something that ceremonial magicians do. In your place, I'd try using the glass glue and see how it works.

As for the gods, it's standard occult teaching that the gods were once what we are now, just as we were once what single-celled organisms are now. There's a constant flow of unformed, unindividualized spirit flowing into manifestation, where it divides up into individual souls that begin working their way back up the long ladder of spiritual evolution. According to some teachings, after immense cycles of manifestation, the souls thus formed eventually return to the Unmanifest...and then the merry-go-round starts up again and away we go. Whee! ;-)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What is the purpose of pathworking? As well as the Tree of Life, what else can you pathwork your way around?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
By Pathworking I assume you mean the process of making a spontaneous imagined journey. It's got three major purposes. The first is that it's the most effective way anyone's come up with to strengthen your ability to visualize (and create forms corresponding to the other senses); it's the bench press of the imagination, if you like. The second is that it teaches you to use the imagination as a sense organ, so that you can perceive nonphysical things directly. The third is that Pathworking on specific symbolic patterns attunes you to the subtle forces behind those patterns, and thus is a very effective tool for self-initiation.

You can do Pathworkings with any symbolic system that has routes going from point A to point B. For example, in some Druid traditions there are six symbolic paths connecting the four elements -- earth to water, earth to air, earth to fire, water to air, water to fire, and air to fire. You can work those Paths in the same way. You can work the Wheel of the Year, or any other sacred calendar.

When there aren't paths to follow, what you're doing is called scrying in th spirit vision. In that, you go through a portal representing a symbol, check out the landscape on the other side, converse with any imaginal beings you meet there, and return. It's nearly as effective as Pathworking for the purposes just named, and you can do it with any symbol whatsoever. Most ceremonial magicians do both, often.

The Ars Notoria from the Lesser Key is useless, since it doesn't come with any of the notae, the magical diagrams that are central to the art, and doesn't cover the principles behind the art. comments.title: Alchemy
comments.content:
Thanks for doing this, John. My apologies if this question comes out rather unclearly--I'm a complete novice in the topic I'd like to ask about.

I was listening to an interview about Taoist alchemy last week. There was much discussion of fusing the cloud spirit and the animal spirit, and my understanding after listening was that the goal is an extension of the person--that is, a Taoist alchemist seeks either an extension of their current life to an age beyond which one could have otherwise expected, or to retain some aspects of their current personality beyond death.

replies.title: Re: Alchemy
replies.content: No, you're not off base. Taoist alchemy is a thing unto itself, and while there are many different schools and systems, a lot of them do focus on avoiding death in one way or another, which is not an important goal of Western alchemy. (The Western alchemical tradition is into healing, and so a successful alchemist in the West should be able to count on a healthy old age, but that's all.) I haven't studied Chinese alchemy in any kind of detail, so don't know what's out there in terms of comparative literature.

replies.title: Re: Birth Control

replies.title: Re: Practical Magic
replies.content:
1) I based the material in the book on the natural magic practiced in Europe during the Middle Ages and Renaissance, which was mostly based in turn on Mediterranean lore from Greek and Roman sources. Pine wasn't important in that magical tradition. That doesn't mean it's not a powerful plant! You'll have to do the hard work of figuring out its symbolism and uses, though.

2) I'd leave it alone. It works, it's popular, don't mess with it -- and magic isn't something to add to something else; it should be its own thing.

3) A lot of ordinary almanacs have bad astrological information -- they don't hire astrologers to do their work, and use rough formulae that don't match the planetary movements when they work out their tables. I've just checked Moontracks.com and their data are quite accurate. What I use for my day-to-day astrology is the Rosicrucian Ephemeris, published by the Rosicrucian Fellowship annually -- it's extremely accurate, and was one of the two most widely used ephemerides back in the pre-computer age.

4) Okay, I'm going to veer off on a tangent. First of all, if your wife has hard tender areas around the edges of her breasts, she's probably wearing bras that are too tight and/or too inflexible. That's very common, and extremely bad for a woman's breast health. Sara quite some years ago started getting her bras from a company called Decent Exposures, which makes soft organic cotton bras which are much less restrictive, and had several longstanding breast problems go away thereafter. Second, I'd encourage you to give your wife daily breast massage, extending into the areas around the breasts where the lymph channels drain away from the breasts; this is also very good for general breast health.

Okay, on to the astrology. When the Moon is in a sign, according to the traditional lore, the part of the body associated with that sign has more blood flow, and so any procedure that could cause bleeding is contraindicated. If it's not something that can cause bleeding, it's not an issue. The name "cancer" is the Latin word for "crab," and the disease got that name because some kinds of tumors, seen at autopsy, spread out from the central mass like crab legs and crab claws. It has nothing to do with the astrological sign.

John--

What exactly is the Left Hand Path? I've seen numerous references to it, but have a difficult time telling it is truly the kind of Miltonian evil-be-thou-my-good variety exercise it appears to be or if there is something more substantial beneath the flamboyant trappings clearly designed to shock.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Religious historian chiming in:

The term comes from Hinduism. In that context the 'right hand path' is that of normal, socially acceptable religious observance. The 'left hand path' is that of tantra - intentionally breaking religious taboos and contemplating death imagery, in order to break free from conditioned responses. [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vamachara]

Sometimes it means roughly the same thing in a western occult context. However other uses imply something closer to the western political implications of right and left: conservative-collectivist vs radical-individualist. In this use the 'right hand path' is about accepting 'nature's way' and your inborn role within it, and the 'left hand path' is about rejecting the status quo and trying to transcend your own nature.

I would point out that the two meanings don't always align - medieval 'memento mori' imagery is morbid but deeply conservative, while transhumanism is deeply radical but with a shiny-upbeat aesthetic. Sometimes they do align, though: I put forward Victor Frankenstein as a left-hand-path archetype who combines both meanings!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Maybe this article on the Qlippoth is what you are looking for.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As our anonymous religious historian has quite accurately pointed out, the moniker was borrowed from Hindu Tantric traditions. Theosophists took to using it as a putdown for traditions they didn't like -- you can still find ominous warnings about "sinister Dugpa sorcery and the Left Hand Path" in old books on magic -- and of course that just inspired people to pick up the label and use it for a variety of more or less antinomian projects. Since those don't interest me, I haven't followed up on the recent mutations of the left hand path scene.

As for the Qlippoth, I cover most of that in Paths of Wisdom, but the article Packshaud cites is also worth reading.

replies.title: Re: Etheric Plane
replies.content:
One material plane explanation may be that you're refreshing the air in the bag of greens every time you interact with it. Plants will continue to use up CO2 and give off O2 for some time after being cut, and this keeps them fresh. A too-tightly sealed bag or plastic container will eventually run out of CO2 and hasten the death of all that tissue. Produce farmers need to be careful to use certain kinds of bags which are designed to slowly vent gases through, in order to extend the shelf life of greens.

Of course, since you presumably breathe in oxygen and breathe out carbon dioxide yourself, there's not a particularly clear line between this materialist explanation and your etheric one. Interacting with your herbs is ticking both those boxes!

comments.title: Central European Current of Magic
comments.content:
In a Well of Galabes post ( https://www.ecosophia.net/blogs-and-essays/the-well-of-galabes/foundations-of-magical-practice-ritual/ ) you briefly describe the "three major currents of ceremonial magic" in the western world:

"Broadly speaking, there’s an English current, which runs from John Dee et al. to the Golden Dawn, with an infusion of Eliphas Levi en route; from there to Dion Fortune and her pupils and associates, of whom Israel Regardie was one, and from there to most modern Anglo-American ceremonial magic. There’s a central European current, which runs from the 18th-century Rosicrucian movement, also with an infusion of Eliphas Levi, through a great many names unfamiliar to my English-speaking readers; the one well known in England and America is Franz Bardon, whose works are of very high quality. Finally, there’s a Traditional current, which has emerged in recent years, and seeks to resurrect such older magical practices as goetic evocation and Renaissance astrological magic."

I have a couple questions about the central European current:

Firstly, I'm wondering what the key differences are between the English current - which I'm pretty familiar with - and the central European current, and what books you might recommend to get more familiar with the latter (aside from Bardon's Initiation into Hermetics, which I own and am currently reading). I currently read only English, but am not averse to learning a second language.

Secondly, would you please comment on some of the differences I've already noticed between Bardon's system in Initiation into Hermetics and the English current in general?:

- Bardon's system seems to get pretty advanced pretty quickly, with exercises like projection of consciousness following on the heels of meditation and pore breathing.

- Bardon's system also seems to lack any basic ritual of any kind, and indeed doesn't seem "ceremonial" in the least. This is curious on its own, but especially because there doesn't seem to be any built-in protection a la the LBRP or SoP.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Central European Current of Magic
replies.content:
1) I haven't followed up on the central European tradition, so can't point you to anything other than Bardon's work and Georg Lomer's Seven Hermetic Letters.

2) Bardon was extremely talented, and one of the downsides is that he didn't include exercises to get from the most basic levels to the advanced stuff. As for the lack of a banishing, that's an issue that pervaded European occultism in the 19th and 20th centuries, and accounts for some of the problems faced by occult groups there.

comments.title: Mormonism
comments.content:
Hi ADJMG!

Are you aware of any connections that Mormonism has to 19th century occultism? Just wondering.

replies.title: Re: Mormonism
replies.content:
There are very strong connections between early Mormonism and Anglophone traditions of magic; Joseph Smith Jr's entire family was actively experimenting with various forms of magic available from printed books, from magical manuscripts, and from oral (folk) traditions in rural Vermont at the time.

(The three northern New England states--and Rhode Island, too--pretty much thumbed their noses at the clerical orthodoxy of Puritan Massachusetts and Connecticut. We have a great deal of evidence for enduring currents of magical practice in all the non-Puritan parts of New England. There is a fair amount of very solid academic scholarship on these currents of magic, published in places off the radar of most occultists and magicians.)

The best book on the subject remains D. Michael Quinn's "Early Mormonism and the Magical World View" (1st ed. 1987; 2nd rev. ed. 1998). For the casual reader, the 2nd edition is better, and has lots of new evidence. The scholar needs both editions, as the 2nd edition also curtailed the bibliography in the 1st edition, in order to make room for the new evidence.

The second best book seems still to be John L. Brooke's "The Refiner's Fire: The Making of Mormon Cosmology" (1994). Brooke downplays the influence of "hands-on" practices like ceremonial magic and scrying, and focuses instead on the (more respectable) intellectual influence of such things as Hermeticism, Alchemy, and Fraternal Orders.

IMHO, the things Brooke emphasizes were less important for both Smith and early Mormonism than the things Quinn emphasizes.

For whatever it may be worth, the oldest surviving example of a black-handled ceremonial magician's ritual knife is still in the possession of descendants of Joseph Jr's Brother Hiram. The choice of magical sigils etched into the blade make it almost completely certain that the knife was forged and etched specifically for use by their father, Joseph Smith Sr., in the practice of magic. [The sigils on the knife, IIRC, go back to Agrippa's "Three Books of Occult Philosophy" (English translation, 1651), probably by way of Barrett's pastiche, "The Magus" (1801).]

Under Brigham Young the magical part of Mormonism got considerably toned down for political reasons, and the toning-down has continued into the present. Mormonism began as a wild and wooly, radical counter-religion to Calvinist orthodoxy, but by now it has morphed into a conservative mainstream form of Christianity (among the Protestant denominations).

replies.title: Re: Mormonism
replies.content:
Hi Robert,
Northern New England boy here. Re: those "enduring currents of magical practice in all the non-Puritan parts of New England," can you point to any places I might start looking?

replies.title: Re: Mormonism
replies.content:
I got started down that scholarly road by following up footnotes in the 1st edition of Quinn's "Early Mormonism," and eventually in Brooke's "Refiner's Fire."

(Do you know about "following up a footnote" as a scholarly practice? You get and read every relevant source cited in each relevant footnote in the work you're reading, then you get and read every relevant source that is cited in each of those earlier works, and so on backwards through the decades and centuries until you can go no further. Usually the trails peter out after about four reiterations of the practice. It tends to build one's expertise rapidly and exponentially.)

Later Michael Bell's "Food for the Dead: On the Trail of New England's Vampires" (2001; 2nd ed. 2011) followed certain other trails of magic through the various primary sources. Quite recently Johannes Dillinger published "Magical Treasure Hunting in Europe and North America: A History" (2012), which opens up yet other, poorly explored trails of magic in old New England.

Peter Benes is also worth reading. His comprehensive "Fortune Tellers, Wise Men, and Magical Healers in New England, 1644-1800" was published in "Wonders of the Invisible World, 1600-1900," a volume which is the Annual Proceedings for 1992 of the Dublin [New Hampshire] Center for New England Folklife. Pretty much every other article in that slim volume is also relevant to your question. You can see the contents (and purchase the book) here:

www.historic-deerfield.org/books-music/wonders-of-the-invisible-world-1600-1900

George Lyman Kittredge's "Witchcraft in Old and New England" (1929) is one of those academic works where a full half of the pages are footnotes in small type to the author's text. But Kittredge's footnotes are gold-mines of information on every sort of magical practice in the English-speaking and German-speaking worlds. It would take you decades to follow up on every lead that he offers. I've been working with Kittredge for about thirty-five years now, and I still haven't exhausted every lead he has to offer.

One thing that seems to me insufficiently emphasized in all these works (even Kittredge's) is the role of wandering magicians and sorcerers from the Pennsylvania German parts of Pennsylvania and Maryland, who actually got as far north as New England as they traveled in search of paying clients. So that's a whole 'nother tradition that cries out for scholarly attention.

replies.title: Re: Mormonism
replies.content:
Robert, thanks so much.
I'm not a scholar and didn't know there was a scholarly name for that practice, but I've certainly ended up deep in rabbitholes of footnotes when I've been interested enough in some question.
I'll be looking into some of these.
Jonathan.

replies.title: Re: Mormonism
replies.content:
Jonathan, you're very welcome! -- I actually don't know whether "following up footnotes" is a standard scholarly term, or even a practice that is taught to graduate students anywhere. My own insatiable curiosity let me to (re)invent for myself during my undergraduate years at UC Berkeley, where I managed to get stack access and spent thousands of hours deep inside them reading everything that seemed interesting and looking for more.

Nowadays no student could manage to do that; vast swaths of every good library's collections are housed off-site, and have to be called up in advance. And (I have recently noticed) libraries are beginning to put caps on how much a student can call up from off-site storage during a specified period of time, probably due to lack of sufficient funding for ample low-level staff.

I'm definitely not the first to ever do this. Kittredge's enormous mass of footnotes show pretty clearly that he did the same thing for decades. He was a Professor of English at Harvard, and the Harvard collections were second to none back in his day.

replies.title: Re: Mormonism
replies.content: Robert got there before me, and cited the two books I was going to name. The short form is that he's quite correct, and the Mormon church is up to its eyeballs in old occult teachings that it's trying to pretend are either not there or are perfectly respectable...

replies.title: Re: question on SOP
